items.q: What is the difference between norms and values?
items.correct: Values are general principles of what is important; norms are specific rules of expected behaviour.
items.misconception: They are two words for exactly the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Norms are laws and values are punishments.
items.why: Students conflate the general guiding ideas (values) with the specific behaviours (norms) that put them into practice.

items.q: What does socialisation mean in sociology?
items.correct: The process of learning the culture, norms and values of one's society.
items.misconception: Simply being sociable and making lots of friends.
items.random_wrong: A purely biological process fixed at birth.
items.why: Learners confuse the everyday sense of being 'social' with the sociological process of learning culture.

items.q: What is the difference between ascribed and achieved status?
items.correct: Ascribed status is given at birth; achieved status is earned through a person's own efforts.
items.misconception: All status is earned through hard work alone.
items.random_wrong: Ascribed status only applies to paid jobs.
items.why: Students assume status is always earned, overlooking positions fixed at birth such as inherited titles.

items.q: Which agent of socialisation is usually described as the 'primary' agent?
items.why: It's The family. “The school” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A status that a person is born with, such as inheriting a royal title, is best described as:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Ascribed status. “Achieved status” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The shared values, customs and way of life of a society are collectively known as its:
items.why: Correct answer: Culture. “Economy” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Specific rules of expected behaviour in a given situation, such as queuing, are called:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Norms. “Values” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
